---
title: "Neuromodulation of Drosophila Flight"

weight: 1
description: "Identifying the specific neural circuits and GABAergic disinhibition required to maintain extended flight bouts in fruit flies."
image: "images/drosophila.jpg" 
categories: ["Neurobiology", "Genetics"]
---

### Project Overview
Insect flight is a complex behavior requiring the real-time integration of multiple sensory inputs with flight motor output. While previous genetic studies identified central brain monoaminergic neurons that modulate flight in *Drosophila melanogaster*, the specific neuro-modulatory circuits underlying sustained, extended flight bouts remained unexplored.

This study focused on how monoaminergic modulation of GABAergic output neurons in the mushroom body (a higher integrating center in the insect brain) regulates the duration of flight bouts.

### Key Findings
* **Circuit Identification:** We discovered that octopaminergic neurons stimulate central dopaminergic neurons, which in turn project to GABAergic mushroom body output neurons (MBONs).
* **The 'Flight State':** Inhibiting these octopaminergic/dopaminergic neurons, or activating the GABAergic MBONs, actively reduces flight duration. We propose that the disinhibition of this neural circuit acts as a "released brake" after flight initiation, allowing the fly to maintain an extended flight state to search for distant food sources or mates.

**Read the full paper:** [Current Biology (2019)](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2018.11.070)
